1: al tismach Yisrael al gil kamimim: 'Do not rejoice, Israel, as other peoples do.' The prohibition against rejoicing at harvest festivals is counterintuitive: harvest was the occasion for covenant celebration (Deuteronomy 16:13-15). But Israel's harvest joy is corrupted: 'you have loved the harlot's wage (etnan zonah) on every threshing floor' — the threshing floor where grain is processed has become the site of cultic prostitution associated with Baal fertility worship. The very place of provision is contaminated by the mode of seeking provision.
2: hagoren vehayeqev lo yir'em: 'The threshing floor and wine press will not provide for them.' The covenantal reversal: the agricultural infrastructure that Baalism claimed to supply will now fail to provide. Vehayayin yechash bah (and the new wine will disappoint her) — kachash is the verb for lying and treachery; the new wine will 'lie' to them, prove false to its promise. The Baal cult promised fertility; YHWH announces its failure. The disappointment of the harvest is the theological argument against Baal in action.
3: lo yeshvu be'Eretz YHWH: 'They will not remain in the LORD's land.' The land belongs to YHWH; residence there is conditional on covenant faithfulness (Leviticus 25:23; Hosea 26:33-38). To be expelled from the land is to be expelled from YHWH's residential domain. 'Ephraim will return to Egypt, and in Assyria they will eat unclean food' — the two exile destinations (Egypt and Assyria) bookend the historical directions of exile; the unclean food reflects the impossibility of maintaining covenant purity outside the land.
4: lo yiseku laYHWH yayin: 'They will not pour out wine offerings to the LORD.' The nesekh (libation) and zevachim (sacrifices) that constitute covenant worship will be impossible in exile — no temple, no proper altar. 'Like mourner's bread (lechem onen), those who eat it will be defiled' — food associated with mourning was ritually unclean (Numbers 19:14; Ezekiel 24:17). The entire food system in exile is contaminated by death-uncleanness; even provision of food cannot be received as covenant blessing when outside the covenant land.
5: mah ta'asu leyom mo'ed uleyom chag YHWH: 'What will you do on the day of the appointed festival, on the day of the LORD's feast?' The rhetorical question forces the exiles to imagine the festival calendar continuing without them. The mo'ed (appointed time/festival) and chag (feast) were the covenant markers of Israel's calendar — Passover, Pentecost, Tabernacles. In exile, these days will arrive without the community being able to celebrate. The question is not about logistical planning but theological confrontation: the festivals that should unite them to YHWH will come and find them absent.
6: ki hinnam halchu mishod Mitzraim tiqabbetzem Mof tikbarem: 'For behold, they are fleeing to escape destruction; Egypt will gather them, and Memphis will bury them.' The refugees who flee to Egypt from Assyrian invasion find not salvation but burial. Mof (Memphis, the Egyptian capital) becomes a cemetery rather than a refuge. 'Their cherished silver, nettles will inherit it; thorns will be in their tents' (ohaleihem) — the personal dwellings that contained their precious things become overgrown ruins. This is the imagery of complete abandonment: the valued objects inherited by weeds.
7: bau yemei hapequdah bau yemei hashillum yeda Yisrael: 'The days of reckoning have come; the days of recompense have arrived. Israel will know it.' Pequddah (visitation, reckoning, inspection) and shillum (recompense, payment) are the terms for covenant justice being executed. The phrase 'Israel will know it' (yeda Yisrael) is ironic: the people who claimed to 'know God' (Hosea 8:2) and had the 'knowledge of God' demanded of them (Hosea 4:6) will now know judgment. 'The prophet is a fool (evil), the man of the spirit is a madman' — either quoting Israel's dismissal of the prophets, or Hosea confessing the absurdity of his prophetic vocation in the community's eyes.
8: tzofeh Efraim im-Elohai navi pach yaqosh al kol-derachav: 'Ephraim's watchman was to stand beside my God, but the prophet has become a fowler's trap on all his paths.' The tzofeh (watchman/sentinel) was the prophet's role in Ezekiel 3:17 and Hosea 33:7 — stationed to give early warning of spiritual danger. Hosea describes how the prophetic office has been perverted: instead of warning against the snares, the prophet has become the snare. Pach yaqosh (fowler's trap) — those who should protect the community from spiritual traps have become the mechanism of entrapment. The corruption of prophetic office is the deepest institutional failure.
9: hiqiqu hishhitu kime Hagi'veah: 'They have gone deep into corruption, as in the days of Gibeah.' Gibeah was the site of the horrific crime of Judges 19-21 — the gang rape and murder of the Levite's concubine that led to the near-extinction of Benjamin. It was the nadir of the judges period, used as a byword for moral atrocity. To say northern Israel has sunk as deep as Gibeah is to locate their sin at the worst point of Israelite history. 'God will remember their iniquity; he will punish their sins' — divine forensic memory leading to enacted judgment.
10: kagavim bamidbar matzati Yisrael: 'I found Israel like grapes in the wilderness.' The discovery of grapes in a desert is the image of startling, unexpected preciousness — Israel was found in an unlikely place (the wilderness of the exodus) and was delightful to YHWH like the first ripe fruit of the fig tree. This is the positive counterpart to the accusation: the covenant relationship had a pure, precious beginning. 'But when they came to Baal-peor, they consecrated themselves to the shameful thing' — Baal-peor (Numbers 25:1-9) is the paradigmatic apostasy, the first significant covenant breach in Canaan. From found-like-grapes to devoted-to-shame: the trajectory of fall from pristine covenant relationship.
11: Efraim kaof yituf kevodo mizzevilada umibetten umihara: 'As for Ephraim, their glory will fly away like a bird — no birth, no pregnancy, no conception.' Kevod (glory) departing like a bird — the same departure of glory that Ezekiel describes from the temple (Ezek 10). The trilogy of reproductive futility (no birth, no pregnancy, no conception — in reverse order of development) represents complete demographic extinction. Ephraim's 'glory' was its population, military strength, and dynastic continuation; all of this flies away. The reversal of the Abrahamic promise (multiply and fill the land) is complete.
12: ki im yegadelu et beneihem veshikaltim me'adam: 'Even if they raise their children, I will bereave them until not one is left.' The worst covenant curse: not merely childlessness but the death of children already raised. Parents invest years in children who are then taken. 'Truly, woe to them when I turn away from them' (gam-oy lahem besuri mehem) — the divine turning-away (suri) is more terrifying than active divine wrath; it is the withdrawal of presence that leaves the community entirely exposed to consequences without any divine buffer.
13: Efraim kasher ra'iti leTzor shatul bena'veh: 'I saw Ephraim planted in a pleasant place, like Tyre.' Tyre was the ancient world's prosperous commercial city, famous for wealth derived from its position. Ephraim similarly occupies a pleasant, productive position. But 'Ephraim must now bring his children out to the slaughterer' (el horegh yotzi banav) — the pleasant position becomes the staging ground for slaughter. The prosperity that should lead to covenant gratitude leads instead to the children's deaths.
14: ten lahem YHWH mah titen ten lahem rechem mashkil veshadadim tzovaim: 'Give them, O LORD — what will you give them? Give them a womb that miscarries and breasts that are dry.' This is one of the OT's most disturbing intercessory prayers — the prophet asks that the worst come quickly rather than slowly. A miscarrying womb and dry breasts means children cannot be born or sustained. The logic: if exile and slaughter await the children, better they not be born. It is a prayer of mercy through the form of a curse — asking for an end to birth so as to prevent the greater horror of children's deaths in the coming judgment.
15: kol ra'atam beGilgal ki sham sene'tim: 'All their evil is at Gilgal — that is where my hatred for them took hold.' Gilgal was where Israel first camped after crossing the Jordan (Joshua 5:10); later it became a cult center associated with apostasy (Amos 4:4; Hosea 5:5). The place of covenant beginning becomes the place where YHWH's hatred (sina'tam, I hated them) took hold. Sina' (hate) in the OT can mean covenantal rejection, the severing of the relationship — not emotional revulsion but covenant repudiation (cf. Malachi 1:3: 'Esau I hated'). The irony: the place of covenant initiation becomes the place of its repudiation.
16: hukah Efraim shorasham yavesh peri val ya'asu: 'Ephraim is struck down; their root is dried up and they will bear no fruit.' The agricultural metaphor: when the root (shoresh) dries up, there is no fruit, however vigorous the branches appeared. Ephraim's root in YHWH — the covenant relationship that was the source of its vitality — has been severed by apostasy; now visible fruitfulness ends. 'Even if they give birth, I will kill their dearly loved children' — the intensification of v12; even fertility that somehow persists will be cut off.
17: yim'asem Elohai ki lo shamevu lo vihiyu novdim bagovim: 'My God will reject them because they did not listen to him; they will become wanderers among the nations.' Ma'as (reject, despise) is the covenant-rejection word — used of YHWH rejecting Saul (1 Samuel 15:23), and here of YHWH rejecting Ephraim. Novdim (wanderers, vagabonds) echoes YHWH's curse on Cain: 'you will be a wanderer on the earth' (Genesis 4:12, na vaved = unstable wanderer). Israel's post-exilic diaspora is the corporate form of the Cain curse. They become the paradigmatic homeless people — the people of the covenant scattered from the covenant land.
